1.1.title: The blind king's loaded question
1.1.body: धृतराष्ट्र frames the entire Gītā with a single लङ् verb: किम् अकुर्वत ("what did they do?") — √कृ, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन past. The सम्बोधन सञ्जय is the addressee, but the real grammatical pivot is मामकाः पाण्डवाः च — "my men AND the Pāṇḍavas," the possessive मामकाः already revealing partisanship. The कर्मधारय धर्मक्षेत्रे कुरुक्षेत्रे layers "field of dharma" over "field of the Kurus," the locatives setting the stage before any agent is named.

1.1.title: युयुत्सवः — desiderative as adjective
1.1.body: युयुत्सवः is a desiderative noun from √युध् ("those desiring to fight"), प्रथमा बहुवचन agreeing with समवेताः. The participle समवेताः (past passive of सम् + √इ) is not the finite verb — it's an attribute. The actual finite verb अकुर्वत waits at the very end, the standard SOV anchor, with everything else orbiting it as qualifier. Already at verse 1, the lesson is: don't mistake a participle for the verb.

1.2.title: Sañjaya's verb cascade — दृष्ट्वा, उपसङ्गम्य, अब्रवीत्
1.2.body: Three actions of Duryodhana telescoped through two absolutives (दृष्ट्वा, उपसङ्गम्य — "having seen," "having approached") leading to one finite verb अब्रवीत् (√ब्रू, लङ्, "he said"). The absolutives mark sequential prior actions; only अब्रवीत् anchors the sentence. व्यूढम् is past passive participle of वि + √व्यह् ("arrayed"), agreeing with पाण्डवानीकम् in द्वितीया एकवचन नपुंसकलिंग.

1.2.title: राजा as appositive irony
1.2.body: दुर्योधनः is the subject; राजा is appositive — Sañjaya calls him "the king" even as he goes seeking reassurance from his teacher. The very next word आचार्यम् (द्वितीया, the object of उपसङ्गम्य) puts Droṇa in the accusative — the king approaches the teacher, not the other way round. The grammar exposes Duryodhana's anxiety: a king who needs to consult his guru before battle begins.

1.3.title: पश्य + एताम् — the imperative that opens the speech
1.3.body: दुर्योधन's first word to Droṇa is पश्य (√दृश्, लोट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन — "see!"). The demonstrative एताम् (द्वितीया स्त्रीलिंग एकवचन) agrees with चमूम् ("army," feminine). The षष्ठी पाण्डुपुत्राणाम् marks possession ("of the sons of Pāṇḍu"), and व्यूढाम् is the past passive participle agreeing with चमूम् — "arrayed by your wise disciple."

1.3.title: The sting in तव शिष्येण धीमता
1.3.body: तृतीया एकवचन शिष्येण with the qualifier धीमता ("wise") is barbed sarcasm aimed at Droṇa: Dhṛṣṭadyumna, born to kill you, is your own student. The तत्पुरुष द्रुपदपुत्रेण ("by Drupada's son") layers in the family vendetta. दुर्योधन is using grammar — the तृतीया of agent — to twist the knife: the army is arrayed by your disciple, against you.

1.4.title: अत्र — the locative that opens the catalog
1.4.body: The अव्यय अत्र ("here") launches Duryodhana's enemy-roll-call. भीमार्जुनसमाः is a compact बहुव्रीहि-कर्मधारय: "those equal-to-Bhīma-and-Arjuna," प्रथमा बहुवचन agreeing with शूराः. The locative युधि ("in battle") restricts the comparison — they are equal to Bhīma and Arjuna specifically in war. महेष्वासाः is also बहुव्रीहि: "those whose bows (इष्वास) are great."

1.4.title: No finite verb — a nominal verse
1.4.body: Remarkably, verse 4 has no finite verb at all. It is a long नामवाक्य (nominal sentence) — a list held together by implied अस्ति. Sanskrit permits this when the predicate is mere existence/identification ("here are heroes..."). The grammar mirrors the content: Duryodhana is enumerating, not narrating. The actual verbal action will resume at verse 7 (निबोध) and 11 (अभिरक्षन्तु).

1.5.title: वीर्यवान् — the matchstick adjective
1.5.body: वीर्यवान् ("possessing valour," मतुप् suffix on वीर्य) appears twice in this stretch (5 and 6), a प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with successive heroes. Note काशिराजः is तत्पुरुष ("king of Kāśī"), while नरपुङ्गवः is कर्मधारय ("bull-among-men," a metaphorical compound). The verse continues the nominal-list mode begun in verse 4 — still no finite verb.

1.5.title: Stacking proper nouns by च
1.5.body: The connector च appears three times (काशिराजश्च, कुन्तिभोजश्च, शैब्यश्च) — the standard द्वंद्व-like joining without a formal compound. Sanskrit can either compress (पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजौ as a दुर्द्वंद्व) or list with repeated च; Duryodhana chooses the latter to draw out each name. The grammar makes the catalog feel inventoried, slow, deliberate — the rhetoric of someone counting threats.

1.6.title: सौभद्रः, द्रौपदेयाः — taddhita patronymics
1.6.body: सौभद्रः is a तद्धित (secondary derivative) from सुभद्रा — "son of Subhadrā," i.e., Abhimanyu. द्रौपदेयाः similarly means "sons of Draupadī" (बहुवचन — all five). Duryodhana names them by their mothers, not their fathers — perhaps because their fathers (the Pāṇḍavas) are too prominent to need naming, or because he wants to flag the matrilineal complications.

1.6.title: सर्व एव महारथाः — the summarizing predicate
1.6.body: The verse closes with सर्वे एव महारथाः — "all indeed great chariot-warriors." एव is the emphatic अव्यय. महारथाः is a कर्मधारय ("great chariot") functioning as a बहुव्रीहि ("those whose chariot is great" = elite warriors). The plural predicate ties off the list begun in verse 4 — still no finite verb, the entire stretch a sustained nominal description.

1.7.title: निबोध — Duryodhana commands his own teacher
1.7.body: निबोध (नि + √बुध्, लोट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन — "know!") is the first finite verb in three verses, and it is an imperative aimed at Droṇa. The सम्बोधन द्विजोत्तम ("best of twice-born") is honorific, but the verb is blunt: Duryodhana is telling his guru to pay attention. ब्रवीमि (लट्, उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन — "I tell") follows immediately — the king takes over the narration.

1.7.title: संज्ञार्थम् — the dative-purpose compound
1.7.body: संज्ञार्थम् is an अव्ययीभाव compound functioning adverbially ("for the sake of identification"). The -अर्थम् ending indicates purpose — a fixed idiomatic suffix. नायकाः मम सैन्यस्य pairs प्रथमा बहुवचन with षष्ठी एकवचन: "the leaders of my army." The ते at the end is चतुर्थी ("to you") — the dative of the addressee for ब्रवीमि.

1.8.title: भवान् — the honorific second person
1.8.body: भवान् is the polite/respectful equivalent of त्वम्, but takes प्रथम-पुरुष (3rd person) verbs grammatically. Listed first — Duryodhana names Droṇa himself as #1. The compound समितिञ्जयः is an उपपद-तत्पुरुष: समिति ("battle") + ञ्जयः ("conqueror," from √जि), meaning "conqueror of battles" — applied to Kṛpa.

1.8.title: सौमदत्तिः — another taddhita patronymic
1.8.body: सौमदत्तिः = "son of Somadatta" (= Bhūriśravas), formed by vṛddhi-strengthening + -इ suffix, the standard तद्धित pattern for "son of X." The verse continues the nominal list (still no finite verb here — the structure leans on ब्रवीमि from verse 7). तथैव च ("and similarly") is a three-word connector — तथा + एव + च — a typical Gītā cadence-filler.

1.9.title: त्यक्तजीविताः — बहुव्रीहि for the suicidal
1.9.body: त्यक्तजीविताः is a stark बहुव्रीहि: "those by whom life has been abandoned" — i.e., warriors who have already given up on living. The first member त्यक्त- is past passive participle of √त्यज्. मदर्थे is locative-purpose ("for my sake"). Duryodhana is boasting that his men have pre-mortgaged their lives to his cause.

1.9.title: नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः — multi-tier compound
1.9.body: नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः parses as नाना (अव्यय, "various") + शस्त्र ("weapon") + प्रहरणाः ("strikers/those who attack"). The whole is a बहुव्रीहि: "those whose striking is with various weapons." युद्धविशारदाः is similarly तत्पुरुष-flavoured बहुव्रीहि ("skilled in war"). The verse remains nominal — list-mode continues.

1.10.title: अपर्याप्तम् vs पर्याप्तम् — the famous ambiguity
1.10.body: अपर्याप्तम् can mean either "insufficient" or "unlimited" (पर्याप्त = limited/measurable, so अपर्याप्त = immeasurable). Most commentators read Duryodhana's intended sense as "our force is unlimited (huge), guarded by Bhīṣma; theirs is limited (small), guarded by Bhīma." Both अपर्याप्तम् and पर्याप्तम् are प्रथमा एकवचन नपुंसकलिंग agreeing with बलम्.

1.10.title: भीष्माभिरक्षितम् — the elegant tatpuruṣa
1.10.body: भीष्माभिरक्षितम् is तृतीया-तत्पुरुष: "protected by Bhīṣma" (the तृतीया of agent absorbed into the compound). The participle अभिरक्षित (past passive of अभि + √रक्ष्) becomes the second member. The mirror compound भीमाभिरक्षितम् in the second half achieves perfect श्लोक्-symmetry — Bhīṣma protects ours, Bhīma protects theirs, the verse balanced on a chiasm.

1.11.title: अभिरक्षन्तु — the strategic imperative
1.11.body: अभिरक्षन्तु is लोट्, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन ("let them protect!") — the third-person imperative, addressed to भवन्तः ("you all," honorific plural taking 3rd-person verbs). भीष्मम् एव — the एव is emphatic: "Bhīṣma specifically." Duryodhana's military instruction surfaces as a grammatical command, the लोट् ending exposing his anxiety about his grandfather's safety.

1.11.title: यथाभागम् — distributive अव्ययीभाव
1.11.body: यथाभागम् is a classic अव्ययीभाव ("according to share/position") — यथा + भाग, the indeclinable functioning adverbially. अयनेषु सर्वेषु is सप्तमी बहुवचन ("in all the entry-points"). अवस्थिताः is past passive participle of अव + √स्था, agreeing with the implied भवन्तः. The sentence is logistically precise: each of you, at your assigned post, must guard Bhīṣma.

1.12.title: सञ्जनयन् — the शतृ participle as cause
1.12.body: सञ्जनयन् is the शतृ (present active participle) of सं + √जन् causative, "producing/generating." Agreeing with कुरुवृद्धः (प्रथमा एकवचन पुल्लिंग), it expresses simultaneous action: "generating joy (हर्षं) for him (तस्य, षष्ठी), he blew the conch." The finite verb is दध्मौ (√ध्मा, लिट् perfect — "he blew"), the perfect tense marking the great deed.

1.12.title: विनद्य — absolutive of the lion-roar
1.12.body: विनद्य is the ल्यप् absolutive of वि + √नद् ("having roared") — used after a prefixed verb instead of -त्वा. सिंहनादम् ("lion-roar") is the द्वितीया object. The sequence is: having roared the lion-roar loudly (उच्चैः, अव्यय), he blew the conch. Bhīṣma performs two acts — the roar, then the blast — but only दध्मौ is finite; विनद्य frames it as prior.

1.13.title: अभ्यहन्यन्त — passive aorist on the battlefield
1.13.body: अभ्यहन्यन्त is लङ् passive, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन of अभि + √हन् — "they were struck/sounded." The instruments themselves are the grammatical subject in प्रथमा बहुवचन (शङ्खाः, भेर्यः, पणवानकगोमुखाः). Sanskrit uses passive readily where English would say "X struck the drums" — here "the drums were struck," with the agent suppressed. The सहसा (अव्यय, "suddenly") + एव intensifies the abruptness.

1.13.title: तुमुलः — the tumult predicated
1.13.body: स शब्दः तुमुलः अभवत् — "that sound became tumultuous." अभवत् is लङ् of √भू, उत्तम-... actually प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन ("became"). तुमुलः is the predicate adjective, प्रथमा एकवचन. Notice how the verse splits cleanly into two halves: first the instruments sounded (passive), then the resulting sound became a roar (active intransitive). The grammar paces the cause-and-effect.

1.14.title: स्थितौ — dual past participle of standing
1.14.body: स्थितौ is प्रथमा द्विवचन past participle of √स्था, agreeing with the द्विवचन subjects माधवः पाण्डवश्च. The locative महति स्यन्दने (सप्तमी एकवचन — "in the great chariot") locates them. श्वेतैः हयैः युक्ते is तृतीया-locative chain — "yoked with white horses," the past participle युक्ते agreeing with स्यन्दने. The whole verse builds visual layers around the dual subjects.

1.14.title: प्रदध्मतुः — dual perfect, dual conch
1.14.body: प्रदध्मतुः is लिट् (perfect), प्रथम पुरुष द्विवचन of प्र + √ध्मा — "the two blew." The द्विवचन subject (माधवः पाण्डवश्च) demands द्विवचन verb, द्विवचन adjective (दिव्यौ), द्विवचन object (शङ्खौ). Sanskrit's preserved dual lets the entire verse march in pairs — Krishna and Arjuna, two divine conches, both blown together. The numerical agreement is itself an aesthetic device.

1.15.title: Three conches, three epithets
1.15.body: Three conches are named in द्वितीया एकवचन: पाञ्चजन्यम्, देवदत्तम्, पौण्ड्रम् — each as object of दध्मौ (which appears once, governing all three via successive subjects). The conch-names match the blower's epithet: हृषीकेशः (Krishna, lord of senses) gets पाञ्चजन्य; धनञ्जयः (wealth-conqueror Arjuna) gets देवदत्त; भीमकर्मा वृकोदरः (Bhīma) gets पौण्ड्र. The grammar threads three subject-object pairs through a single perfect verb.

1.15.title: भीमकर्मा, वृकोदरः — Bhīma's two बहुव्रीहि
1.15.body: भीमकर्मा = "he whose deeds are terrible" (बहुव्रीहि), and वृकोदरः = "he whose belly is wolf-like" (another बहुव्रीहि). Two epithets stacked, both agreeing with the implied subject. महाशङ्खम् is कर्मधारय ("great conch"). Sanskrit packs three identifications of Bhīma into a single pāda — name suppressed, body and deed displayed.

1.16.title: अनन्तविजयम् — the king's conch
1.16.body: अनन्तविजयम् ("endless victory") is a कर्मधारय functioning as a proper noun, द्वितीया एकवचन — Yudhiṣṭhira's conch. The subject राजा कुन्तीपुत्रः युधिष्ठिरः stacks three appositives in प्रथमा एकवचन (king + son-of-Kuntī + Yudhiṣṭhira). The verb दध्मौ is again elided after verse 15, governed across the verses by extension.

1.16.title: Dual subject, dual conch — Nakula and Sahadeva
1.16.body: नकुलः सहदेवश्च is grammatically singular+singular joined by च, but functions as a dual pair, taking the द्विवचन object सुघोषमणिपुष्पकौ — a द्वंद्व compound: सुघोष ("sweet-sound") + मणिपुष्पक ("jewel-flower"). The द्वंद्व of two conch-names matches the pairing of the twins. Sanskrit's compound system lets the verse fuse two names into one द्विवचन accusative form.

1.17.title: परमेष्वासः, महारथः, अपराजितः — three epithet-styles
1.17.body: Three different epithet-formations applied to three warriors: परमेष्वासः is बहुव्रीहि ("whose bow is supreme"), महारथः is बहुव्रीहि ("whose chariot is great"), अपराजितः is past passive participle with privative अ- ("unconquered"). The verse is grammatically a list (still no main verb — दध्मुः waits at 18), but each name carries a stylistically distinct qualifier.

1.17.title: च as the rhythm-keeper
1.17.body: च appears four times in this short verse, marking the additive flow: काश्यश्च, शिखण्डी च, विराटश्च, सात्यकिश्चापराजितः. Sanskrit could compress with द्वंद्व but instead chooses serial च for ceremonial effect — the way a roll-call sounds when read aloud. The grammar lets the verse breathe between each name, befitting the catalog of allies.

1.18.title: दध्मुः — the long-deferred finite verb
1.18.body: दध्मुः (लिट्, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन of √ध्मा — "they blew") finally arrives as the finite verb governing verses 17 and 18 together. पृथक्पृथक् (अव्यय, "each separately") is a reduplicated indeclinable adverb. शङ्खान् is द्वितीया बहुवचन object. The सम्बोधन पृथिवीपते ("O lord of earth") addresses Dhṛtarāṣṭra, reminding us of the narrative frame.

1.18.title: द्रौपदेयाः — five sons in one प्रथमा बहुवचन
1.18.body: द्रौपदेयाः is the taddhita patronymic in बहुवचन — "the five sons of Draupadī" rolled into one nominative plural. सर्वशः (अव्यय, "in every way / completely") modifies the action. The verse ties off the long Pāṇḍava-side roll-call begun at 14, with दध्मुः as the suspended verb that finally activates all those subjects.

1.19.title: व्यदारयत् — the sound tore hearts
1.19.body: व्यदारयत् is लङ् causative of वि + √दृ ("split, rend"), प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — "caused to be torn." The subject is स घोषः ("that sound"); the object हृदयानि ("hearts," द्वितीया बहुवचन नपुंसक). The षष्ठी धार्तराष्ट्राणाम् ("of Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons") marks possession. Causative morphology turns sound into agent of violence — the verb does work that English needs a metaphor for.

1.19.title: व्यनुनादयन् — present participle as continuation
1.19.body: व्यनुनादयन् is शतृ (present active participle) of वि + अनु + √नद् causative — "causing to resound." It agrees with तुमुलः ("the tumult") in प्रथमा एकवचन. नभश्च पृथिवीं चैव are द्वितीया objects of the participle's transitive force. The verse's structure: finite verb (व्यदारयत्) describes effect on enemy hearts; participle (व्यनुनादयन्) describes ongoing reverberation through sky and earth — two scales of impact.

1.20.title: Three absolutive/participle setups, no main verb yet
1.20.body: Verse 20 is grammatically open-ended — it sets up the action but the finite verb arrives in verse 21 (आह). दृष्ट्वा (absolutive), प्रवृत्ते (locative absolute — "weapons-clash having begun"), उद्यम्य (absolutive — "having raised") — three subordinate actions chained. कपिध्वजः ("monkey-bannered") is a बहुव्रीहि epithet for Arjuna, his banner bearing Hanumān.

1.20.title: प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसम्पाते — सति-सप्तमी (locative absolute)
1.20.body: प्रवृत्ते शस्त्रसम्पाते is the classic Sanskrit सति-सप्तमी construction: both noun and participle in सप्तमी एकवचन, expressing a circumstance ("when the weapons-clash had begun"). शस्त्रसम्पातः is तत्पुरुष ("clash of weapons"). The locative absolute is Sanskrit's elegant alternative to a temporal clause — no "when" needed, the case marking does the work.

1.21.title: आह — the historical perfect of speaking
1.21.body: आह is परोक्ष भूतकाल (perfect) of √ब्रू, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — "he said." An archaic, defective verb used precisely for solemn or reported speech. हृषीकेशम् is द्वितीया एकवचन (Krishna as object of address). वाक्यम् इदम् is द्वितीया एकवचन ("this speech") — the object spoken. The सम्बोधन महीपते addresses Dhṛtarāṣṭra in the frame, while अच्युत inside the speech addresses Krishna.

1.21.title: स्थापय — Arjuna's first imperative
1.21.body: स्थापय (लोट्, मध्यम पुरुष एकवचन of √स्था causative — "cause to stand!"). The सप्तमी सेनयोः उभयोः मध्ये ("between the two armies") locates the requested action. मे (षष्ठी or चतुर्थी एकवचन — "my" / "for me") is the enclitic pronoun. Arjuna's voice enters the Gītā in the imperative — a command issued to Krishna, who will become his teacher.

1.22.title: यावत् + लट् — "until I survey"
1.22.body: यावत् is a temporal अव्यय meaning "as long as / until," pairing with the लट् verb निरीक्षे (आत्मनेपद, उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन of निर् + √ईक्ष् — "I survey"). The construction means "until I look at these." योद्धुकामान् is बहुव्रीहि ("those whose desire is to fight" — योद्धुम् + काम), द्वितीया बहुवचन agreeing with the object.

1.22.title: योद्धव्यम् — कृत्य participle as gerundive
1.22.body: योद्धव्यम् is the तव्यत् कृत्य (gerundive/future passive participle) of √युध् — "to-be-fought," प्रथमा एकवचन नपुंसकलिंग. The construction कैः मया सह योद्धव्यम् is impersonal-passive: "by whom (तृतीया कैः) is it to-be-fought with-me (मया सह)?" Sanskrit gerundives turn into impersonal passives this way — agent in तृतीया, no nominative subject. रणसमुद्यमे ("in this battle-undertaking") is locative.

1.23.title: योत्स्यमानान् — future middle participle
1.23.body: योत्स्यमानान् is the शानच् (present middle participle) built on the future stem of √युध् (योत्स्य- + मान), द्वितीया बहुवचन — "those who will be fighting / those about to fight." Sanskrit allows participles on any tense-stem, including the future. अवेक्षे is लट् आत्मनेपद उत्तम एकवचन of अव + √ईक्ष् ("I look at").

1.23.title: प्रियचिकीर्षवः — desiderative inside a बहुव्रीहि
1.23.body: प्रियचिकीर्षवः is a richly layered compound: चिकीर्षु is the desiderative noun from √कृ ("one wishing to do"), and प्रिय ("dear, pleasing") modifies what they wish to do — "those wishing to do what-is-pleasing." The षष्ठी धार्तराष्ट्रस्य दुर्बुद्धेः ("of evil-minded Duryodhana") tells whose pleasure. दुर्बुद्धेः is itself a बहुव्रीहि ("whose intellect is evil").

1.24.title: Sañjaya resumes — एवम् उक्तः
1.24.body: एवम् उक्तः is the standard Sanskrit "thus addressed" — past passive participle in प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with हृषीकेशः. The agent गुडाकेशेन ("by Guḍākeśa = conqueror-of-sleep = Arjuna," तृतीया एकवचन). सम्बोधन भारत addresses Dhṛtarāṣṭra. The construction is passive: "Hṛṣīkeśa, thus addressed by Arjuna...."

1.24.title: स्थापयित्वा — absolutive, no main verb yet
1.24.body: स्थापयित्वा (absolutive of √स्था causative — "having placed") leaves verse 24 syntactically incomplete: "having placed the chariot..." — the finite verb (उवाच) waits at verse 25. The सेनयोः उभयोः मध्ये locative (sapt-ī dual) echoes Arjuna's request verbatim from 21. रथोत्तमम् is कर्मधारय द्वितीया एकवचन — "the best chariot."

1.25.title: उवाच — perfect of solemn utterance
1.25.body: उवाच is लिट् of √वच्, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — "he said." The perfect tense (as opposed to imperfect लङ् or aorist लुङ्) marks great or distant speech. भीष्मद्रोणप्रमुखतः is an अव्यय-like compound ("in front of Bhīṣma and Droṇa") using -तस् ablative-adverbial suffix. इति closes the quotation.

1.25.title: पश्य + एतान् — Krishna's first words
1.25.body: Krishna's first imperative in the Gītā is पश्य ("see!") + एतान् समवेतान् कुरून् ("these gathered Kurus," all द्वितीया बहुवचन). The सम्बोधन पार्थ pairs the imperative. There is dark symmetry: Duryodhana opened the chapter at 1.3 with पश्य to Droṇa about the Pāṇḍava army; now Krishna says पश्य to Arjuna about the Kuru army. The same verb, opposite gazes.

1.26.title: अपश्यत् — what Arjuna saw
1.26.body: अपश्यत् is लङ् of √दृश्, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — "he saw." तत्र ("there," अव्यय) sets the scene. स्थितान् (past passive participle of √स्था, द्वितीया बहुवचन पुल्लिंग) agrees with the long list of relatives in द्वितीया बहुवचन: पितॄन्, पितामहान्, आचार्यान्, मातुलान्, भ्रातॄन्, पुत्रान्, पौत्रान्, सखीन्. The grammar accumulates the catastrophe one accusative at a time.

1.26.title: Pure द्वितीया cascade — the catalog of grief
1.26.body: Verse 26 is a study in द्वितीया बहुवचन morphology across kinship terms: -ॄन् (पितॄन्, भ्रातॄन्) for ृ-stems, -आन् (पुत्रान्, पौत्रान्) for अ-stems, -ईन् (सखीन्) for the irregular सखि-stem. Sanskrit grammar lays out the entire kinship structure on its case-system — and Arjuna's eye sweeps it accusative-by-accusative.

1.27.title: समीक्ष्य — the absolutive that triggers despair
1.27.body: समीक्ष्य is ल्यप् absolutive of सम् + √ईक्ष् ("having looked at") — used because of the prefix सम्. The object तान् सर्वान् बन्धून् अवस्थितान् ("all those gathered relatives") is in द्वितीया बहुवचन. कौन्तेयः is taddhita patronymic ("son of Kuntī") agreeing with स (Arjuna). The finite verb is suspended — अब्रवीत् arrives in verse 28.

1.27.title: सेनयोः उभयोः अपि — the dual genitive with अपि
1.27.body: सेनयोः उभयोः is षष्ठी द्विवचन ("of both armies"), with अपि ("even / also") emphasizing the totality. Sanskrit's dual is mandatory for naturally paired entities; उभयोः intensifies it as "both of the two." The relatives are not on one side — they are on both, which is the grammatical anchor of Arjuna's tragedy.

1.28.title: विषीदन् — the present participle of collapse
1.28.body: विषीदन् is शतृ of वि + √सद् ("sinking, despairing") — प्रथमा एकवचन पुल्लिंग agreeing with the implied Arjuna. कृपया परया आविष्टः ("overcome by extreme pity") uses तृतीया कृपया as agent of आविष्टः (past passive participle of आ + √विश्, "entered/overcome"). The finite verb अब्रवीत् ("he said") finally surfaces, having been suspended since verse 26.

1.28.title: युयुत्सुम् — desiderative again, now ironic
1.28.body: Arjuna calls his own kin युयुत्सुम् ("desirous-of-fighting," desiderative noun from √युध्) — द्वितीया एकवचन agreeing with स्वजनम्. The same form युयुत्सवः opened the entire Gītā at 1.1, describing all the warriors. Here it shrinks down to qualify Arjuna's own family — and that compression is what undoes him. समुपस्थितम् is past passive participle ("approached, arrived").

1.29.title: Four parallel लट् verbs of bodily collapse
1.29.body: Arjuna's body is described in four simultaneous लट् verbs: सीदन्ति (√सद्, प्र-पु बहुवचन — "sink," with गात्राणि as subject), परिशुष्यति (परि + √शुष् — "dries up," with मुखम् as subject), उत्पन्न... actually वेपथुः is just a noun ("trembling") in implied predicate, and जायते (√जन् ātmanepada — "is born/arises"). Subject changes verb-by-verb; the body fragments grammatically.

1.29.title: रोमहर्षः — कर्मधारय for goosebumps
1.29.body: रोमहर्षः ("hair-thrill") is a कर्मधारय: रोम ("body-hair") + हर्ष ("bristling/joy"). The noun's etymology assumes hair stands up in either ecstasy or terror — Sanskrit lexicalizes the physical reaction itself. Paired with वेपथुः ("trembling," formed with kṛt suffix -अथु on √वेप्), the verse compiles a clinical inventory of fear, in clean Sanskrit grammar.

1.30.title: स्रंसते — the bow slipping in middle voice
1.30.body: स्रंसते is लट् ātmanepada of √स्रंस्, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — "slips, falls." The middle voice is key: the bow slips of-its-own-accord (not "someone drops it"). गाण्डीवम् is प्रथमा एकवचन subject; हस्तात् is पञ्चमी एकवचन ("from the hand"). The fifth case marks the source of the falling. Sanskrit's middle voice expresses precisely this autonomous, agentless slippage — Arjuna's grip fails on its own.

1.30.title: भ्रमति इव — the simile of a turning mind
1.30.body: भ्रमति is लट् of √भ्रम् ("whirls"), with the अव्यय इव ("as if") softening it to a simile: "my mind whirls, as it were." मे मनः: षष्ठी एकवचन + प्रथमा एकवचन ("my mind"). The neighboring शक्नोमि (लट् of √शक्, उत्तम एकवचन) + अवस्थातुम् (infinitive of अव + √स्था — "to stand") is a classic Sanskrit modal: "I am not able to stand."

1.31.title: निमित्तानि विपरीतानि — omens reversed
1.31.body: निमित्तानि is प्रथमा बहुवचन ("omens, signs"); विपरीतानि agrees with it (past passive participle of वि + परि + √इ — "reversed, inverted"). पश्यामि (लट्, उत्तम एकवचन — "I see"). Then a separate clause: न... अनुपश्यामि (अनु + √दृश् — "I do not foresee") with श्रेयः (नपुंसक प्रथमा, "good") as its object. The verse balances seeing-bad with not-seeing-good.

1.31.title: हत्वा स्वजनम् आहवे — the absolutive of objection
1.31.body: हत्वा is the absolutive of √हन् ("having killed") — Arjuna deploys it to express the conditional: "by killing one's own people in battle." स्वजनम् is द्वितीया एकवचन (the slain), आहवे is सप्तमी एकवचन ("in war"). The absolutive here functions almost like an English gerund ("from killing") — the action that he refuses to foresee any good from.

1.32.title: न काङ्क्षे — the verb of renunciation
1.32.body: काङ्क्षे is लट् ātmanepada of √काङ्क्ष् ("I desire"), उत्तम पुरुष एकवचन. With न it becomes "I do not desire." Three objects in द्वितीया एकवचन/बहुवचन: विजयम्, राज्यम्, सुखानि — victory, kingship, pleasures. The single verb governs all three. The सम्बोधन कृष्ण softens the bluntness of the renunciation.

1.32.title: किं नो राज्येन — the तृतीया of useless purpose
1.32.body: किम् + तृतीया is the classic Sanskrit idiom "what is the use of X?" — किं नो राज्येन = "what to us with kingship?" The instrumental राज्येन marks the means whose utility is being questioned. Repeated with भोगैः जीवितेन वा ("with enjoyments or life"). नः is enclitic चतुर्थी / षष्ठी ("to us / our"). The grammar of dismissal is built on the third case.

1.33.title: येषाम् अर्थे — षष्ठी governed by अर्थे
1.33.body: येषाम् अर्थे ("for whose sake") uses षष्ठी बहुवचन येषाम् with the locative अर्थे ("for the purpose"). The relative pronoun येषाम् sets up a long correlation with ते इमे ("these very ones") in the second half. काङ्क्षितम् नः is past passive participle of √काङ्क्ष् with षष्ठी/चतुर्थी enclitic नः ("desired by us").

1.33.title: प्राणान् त्यक्त्वा धनानि च — the absolutive of total surrender
1.33.body: त्यक्त्वा (absolutive of √त्यज् — "having abandoned") governs two द्वितीया objects: प्राणान् ("life-breaths") and धनानि ("wealth"). The absolutive marks prior, completed action: these relatives are already-having-abandoned everything to stand here. Arjuna's despair is sharpened by recognizing the cost they have already paid before the first arrow flies.

1.34.title: Six kinship nominatives, no verb
1.34.body: Verse 34 is pure nominal listing in प्रथमा बहुवचन: आचार्याः, पितरः, पुत्राः, पितामहाः, मातुलाः, श्वशुराः, पौत्राः, श्यालाः, सम्बन्धिनः. No verb — the verse hangs as appositive expansion of ते इमे from verse 33. The grammar mimics how a glance across a battlefield catalogs faces, naming without acting.

1.34.title: श्यालाः, सम्बन्धिनः — the extended family
1.34.body: श्यालाः is प्रथमा बहुवचन of श्याल ("brother-in-law," wife's brother). सम्बन्धिनः ("connections") is the catch-all relational noun in प्रथमा बहुवचन, formed with -इन् suffix on सम्बन्ध. Sanskrit kinship terminology is so granular that even after eight categories, a tenth ("others connected") is needed — and Arjuna names them all.

1.35.title: हन्तुम् इच्छामि — infinitive + main verb of desire
1.35.body: हन्तुम् is the तुमुन् infinitive of √हन् ("to kill"). इच्छामि is लट् उत्तम एकवचन of √इष् ("I wish"). The pattern: infinitive as object of a desire-verb. With न it negates: "I do not wish to kill." एतान् is द्वितीया बहुवचन ("these"). The सम्बोधन मधुसूदन ("slayer of Madhu") is provocative — Arjuna addresses the killer-god while refusing to kill.

1.35.title: घ्नतः अपि — concessive present participle in षष्ठी
1.35.body: घ्नतः is शतृ participle of √हन् ("killing") in षष्ठी एकवचन — but here probably functioning as concessive: "even of those who are killing [us]." अपि (अव्यय) marks concession ("even though"). The whole second half is a fortiori reasoning: "not even for the kingdom of three worlds, how then for earth (महीकृते — चतुर्थी / दात-locative-purpose)."

1.36.title: निहत्य — absolutive of slaughter
1.36.body: निहत्य is ल्यप् absolutive of नि + √हन् ("having killed thoroughly"). The conditional sense: "having killed Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons, what joy would there be for us?" का प्रीतिः स्यात् uses विधिलिङ् of √अस् ("would be") — the optative for hypothetical consequences. नः is चतुर्थी enclitic ("for us").

1.36.title: आश्रयेत् — विधिलिङ् of sin attaching
1.36.body: आश्रयेत् is विधिलिङ् of आ + √श्रि ("would attach to, would resort to"), प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन. The subject पापम् एव ("sin alone," with emphatic एव). अस्मान् द्वितीया ("us") as object. आततायिनः is द्वितीया बहुवचन — "the aggressors" (taddhita-derivative of आततायिन्). The विधिलिङ् here is the conditional consequence-mood: "sin alone would-attach to us."

1.37.title: नार्हाः — predicate adjective of non-fitness
1.37.body: अर्ह is from √अर्ह् ("to be worthy"), here as predicate प्रथमा बहुवचन with negative न and the infinitive हन्तुम् ("to kill"). The construction means "we are not worthy/fit to kill." वयम् is प्रथमा बहुवचन ("we"). स्वबान्धवान् (कर्मधारय "own-relatives") is the द्वितीया object.

1.37.title: कथं स्याम — विधिलिङ् in rhetorical incredulity
1.37.body: स्याम is विधिलिङ् उत्तम बहुवचन of √अस् — "would we be." The conditional mood plus कथम् ("how?") makes the rhetorical question of impossibility. सुखिनः (प्रथमा बहुवचन of सुखिन् with -इन् suffix — "happy ones") as predicate. हत्वा (absolutive) supplies the condition: "having killed our kin, how could we be happy?"

1.38.title: यद्यपि — concessive conjunction
1.38.body: यद्यपि = यदि + अपि ("even if"), a fixed concessive अव्यय. एते ("these," Duryodhana's faction) is प्रथमा बहुवचन. पश्यन्ति is लट् of √दृश् ("they see"); न पश्यन्ति is the negated form. लोभोपहतचेतसः is a long बहुव्रीहि: लोभ-उपहत-चेतस् = "those whose minds (चेतस्) have been struck-down (उपहत) by greed (लोभ)."

1.38.title: Two द्वितीया objects awaiting the verb
1.38.body: कुलक्षयकृतं दोषम् ("the fault caused by clan-destruction") and मित्रद्रोहे पातकम् ("sin in friend-treachery") are two द्वितीया objects of न पश्यन्ति. कुलक्षयकृतम् is उपपद-तत्पुरुष: "made by destruction-of-clan." The verse alone is incomplete — its main clause arrives at verse 39 (कथं न ज्ञेयम्).

1.39.title: ज्ञेयम् — कृत्य participle as duty
1.39.body: ज्ञेयम् is the यत् कृत्य (gerundive/passive future participle) of √ज्ञा — "to-be-known," प्रथमा एकवचन नपुंसकलिंग. The construction कथं न ज्ञेयम् अस्माभिः is impersonal-passive: "how is it not to-be-known by us (तृतीया अस्माभिः)?" Sanskrit's gerundive again routes agency into the तृतीया.

1.39.title: निवर्तितुम् — infinitive of withdrawal
1.39.body: निवर्तितुम् is the तुमुन् infinitive of नि + √वृत् ("to turn back"). The ablative पापात् अस्मात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — "from this sin") marks what one withdraws from — the fifth case of motion-away. प्रपश्यद्भिः is शतृ-तृतीया बहुवचन ("by those seeing"), agent of ज्ञेयम्. The grammar dovetails three constructions: gerundive + infinitive + ablative-of-withdrawal.

1.40.title: प्रणश्यन्ति, अभिभवति — two pivotal लट् verbs
1.40.body: प्रणश्यन्ति (प्र + √नश्, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन — "perish") with subject कुलधर्माः सनातनाः (प्रथमा बहुवचन — "the eternal clan-dharmas"). The locative absolute कुले क्षये (sapt-ī "in clan-destruction") sets the condition. The second clause uses धर्मे नष्टे (locative absolute — "when dharma is destroyed") and the verb अभिभवति (अभि + √भू — "overpowers," प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन).

1.40.title: उत — the particle of certainty
1.40.body: उत is a small but loaded अव्यय meaning "indeed, certainly" — used to seal a logical consequence. कुलं कृत्स्नम् ("the entire clan," कर्मधारय द्वितीया एकवचन) is the object of अभिभवति. अधर्मः is its subject. The verse's structure is two locative absolutes feeding into two finite consequences — Sanskrit's compressed conditional logic on display.

1.41.title: प्रदुष्यन्ति — present of moral corruption
1.41.body: प्रदुष्यन्ति is प्र + √दुष्, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन — "become corrupted." Subject कुलस्त्रियः ("clan-women," प्रथमा बहुवचन). The cause is in पञ्चमी: अधर्माभिभवात् ("from adharma's prevailing") — ablative of cause. Sanskrit's fifth case carries both motion-away and causal-because; here causal.

1.41.title: स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु — locative absolute of consequence
1.41.body: स्त्रीषु दुष्टासु is सति-सप्तमी again: सप्तमी बहुवचन of स्त्री + past participle दुष्टा — "when women are corrupted." जायते (लट् ātmanepada of √जन् — "is born") follows; subject वर्णसङ्करः ("varṇa-mixture," तत्पुरुष). The सम्बोधन वार्ष्णेय ("O Vārṣṇeya") slips in — Krishna addressed as descendant of Vṛṣṇi clan, the clan-anxiety surfacing in the very vocative.

1.42.title: नरकाय एव — चतुर्थी of destination
1.42.body: नरकाय is चतुर्थी एकवचन ("for/to hell") with the emphatic एव. The dative-of-destination here means "leads only to hell." The implicit verb is भवति. कुलघ्नानाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन of कुलघ्न "clan-killer") and कुलस्य च (षष्ठी एकवचन "of the clan") tell whose destination — both perpetrator and family.

1.42.title: लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः — the four-piece बहुव्रीहि
1.42.body: लुप्तपिण्डोदकक्रियाः parses as लुप्त ("vanished") + पिण्ड ("rice-ball") + उदक ("water") + क्रिया ("rite") — a बहुव्रीहि agreeing with पितरः ("ancestors," प्रथमा बहुवचन): "those whose rice-and-water rites have vanished." पतन्ति (लट् of √पत्, प्रथम-पुरुष बहुवचन — "they fall") is the finite verb. Sanskrit's compound compresses an entire ritual sociology into one adjective.

1.43.title: उत्साद्यन्ते — causative passive
1.43.body: उत्साद्यन्ते is लट् passive (yak-suffix) of उत् + √सद् causative, प्रथम पुरुष बहुवचन — "are caused to be destroyed." The morphology: root + ṇic (causative) + yak (passive) + present endings. Subject जातिधर्माः कुलधर्माः च (प्रथमा बहुवचन). The तृतीया agent is दोषैः एतैः ("by these faults").

1.43.title: वर्णसङ्करकारकैः — agent of mixing
1.43.body: वर्णसङ्करकारकैः is तृतीया बहुवचन agreeing with दोषैः — "by [faults] which cause varṇa-mixing." The compound is उपपद-तत्पुरुष: "makers of varṇa-mixture." शाश्वताः (प्रथमा बहुवचन — "eternal") qualifies कुलधर्माः. Arjuna's argument: the eternal becomes uprooted by the agentive faults. Causative-passive in the verb mirrors causative compound in the agent.

1.44.title: अनुशुश्रुम — perfect of tradition
1.44.body: अनुशुश्रुम is लिट् (perfect) of अनु + √श्रु, उत्तम पुरुष बहुवचन — "we have heard [in tradition]." The perfect with अनु- carries the sense of "from tradition, from those before us." The इति-quotation marker closes a reported claim. The verse routes truth through ancestral hearing — श्रुति-style epistemology.

1.44.title: अनियतम् — privative compound of indefinite stay
1.44.body: अनियतम् ("un-fixed, indefinite") is अव्यय / नपुंसकलिंग प्रथमा एकवचन, modifying वासः ("dwelling"). नियतम् (past passive of नि + √यम्) becomes अनियतम् with privative अ-. नरके is सप्तमी एकवचन ("in hell"). उत्सन्नकुलधर्माणाम् is षष्ठी बहुवचन ("of those whose clan-dharmas are uprooted") — another बहुव्रीहि. Living-in-hell-indefinitely encoded in five Sanskrit morphemes.

1.45.title: अहो बत — the double interjection
1.45.body: अहो and बत are both अव्यय interjections of grief/wonder, doubled for intensity: "alas, oh!" The literal verse-grammar: महत् पापम् कर्तुम् ("to do great sin," infinitive तुमुन् + द्वितीया object). व्यवसिताः (past passive participle of वि + अव + √सो — "resolved, set") in प्रथमा बहुवचन agreeing with वयम्. The construction means "we are resolved to do great sin."

1.45.title: उद्यताः — past participle of armed readiness
1.45.body: उद्यताः (past passive of उत् + √यम्, "raised, ready") agrees with implicit वयम्. हन्तुम् (तुमुन् infinitive — "to kill") + स्वजनम् (कर्मधारय द्वितीया एकवचन — "own-people"). The तृतीया राज्यसुखलोभेन ("by greed for kingdom-pleasure") is the cause-instrument. Three Sanskrit forms — participle + infinitive + तृतीया — diagnose Arjuna's exact moral position: prepared, to kill, out of greed.

1.46.title: क्षेमतरम् — comparative of safety
1.46.body: क्षेमतरम् is the तरप् comparative of क्षेम ("safe, beneficial") — प्रथमा एकवचन नपुंसकलिंग predicate, agreeing with implied तत् ("that"). भवेत् is विधिलिङ् of √भू ("would be"). Sanskrit's तरप् gives "more X"; here "more beneficial." The conditional yadi ... tat structure is hypothetical: "if they were to kill me unarmed, that would be more beneficial."

1.46.title: अप्रतीकारम्, अशस्त्रम् — two privative द्वितीया
1.46.body: अप्रतीकारम् ("un-retaliating," अ- + प्रतीकार) and अशस्त्रम् ("un-armed") are both द्वितीया एकवचन agreeing with माम् — privative compounds describing Arjuna's chosen vulnerability. हन्युः is विधिलिङ् of √हन्, प्रथम-पुरुष बहुवचन ("they might kill"). शस्त्रपाणयः is बहुव्रीहि: "those whose hands are weapons." The grammar contrasts privative-Arjuna with weapon-armed-enemies in one श्लोक्.

1.47.title: उपाविशत् — the verb of collapse
1.47.body: उपाविशत् is लङ् of उप + आ + √विश्, प्रथम पुरुष एकवचन — "he sat down." The locative रथोपस्थे (सप्तमी एकवचन — "on the chariot-seat") locates the action. एवम् उक्त्वा is the absolutive ("having spoken thus") — sealing the despair-speech that ran 28-46. सङ्ख्ये (सप्तमी एकवचन — "in battle") is the field of action.

1.47.title: विसृज्य and शोकसंविग्नमानसः — the closing setup
1.47.body: विसृज्य is ल्यप् absolutive of वि + √सृज् ("having released"). The object: सशरम् चापम् ("bow with arrow," बहुव्रीही-कर्मधारय द्वितीया एकवचन — स + शर = "having arrows"). शोकसंविग्नमानसः is a final triple compound: शोक + संविग्न + मानस — बहुव्रीहि "whose mind is agitated by grief." Arjuna ends Chapter 1 as he began — but inverted: no longer युयुत्सुः (eager-to-fight), now seated, weapon released, mind shaken. The grammatical mirror is the chapter's structure.
